rationale.AWR: DukeReach/CAPS provide proactive case-management and outreach with multiple referral channels, but no independently audited disaggregated distress monitoring. Signal: established CAPS + Healthy Duke wellbeing infrastructure.
rationale.EMP: CAPS and Office for Institutional Equity exist; no independent ombudsperson with published caseload, and grad-worker accounts of bargaining describe transactional treatment. Signal: DGSU describing administration as adversarial in 2024-25 bargaining.
rationale.ACT: CAPS offers same-day urgent and short-term therapy with referral network; wait-time data not publicly benchmarked. Signal: functioning CAPS but unpublished waitlist data.
rationale.EQU: Strong financial aid (need-blind, meets full need) and Pell access efforts, but adjunct/contingent pay differentials persist. Signal: generous aid vs. contingent-labor gap.
rationale.BND: DGSU ratified its first contract Aug 2025 (99% vote, 6.25% raise, expanded benefits) — a real autonomy/labor gain. Signal: historic first grad-union contract.
rationale.ACC: Title IX office and conduct processes are documented; no recent pattern of self-initiated harm disclosure or major OCR resolution found, but transparency is limited to compliance reporting. Signal: standard compliance posture, no proactive disclosure evidence.
rationale.SYS: Healthy Duke and JED-style wellbeing strategy show some upstream investment; limited public structural advocacy carrying institutional risk. Signal: wellbeing strategy without strong structural critique.
rationale.INT: Doing involuntary layoffs and buyouts (~600) in 2025 amid federal funding cuts while protecting endowment; values-vs-cost tension during pressure. Signal: cost-cutting falling partly on staff during fiscal stress.

rationale.AWR: Counselling and Mental Health Service plus staff wellbeing pages exist; UK-typical proactive systems but no independently audited disaggregation. Signal: established student counselling service.
rationale.EMP: Student support exists but the 2024-25 restructuring and 900+ jobs-at-risk announcement drew UCU criticism of how staff were treated. Signal: union accounts of impersonal restructuring.
rationale.ACT: Counselling service and SU advice exist; UK-sector wait-time pressures and staffing strain reduce responsiveness during cuts. Signal: service present but resourcing under strain.
rationale.EQU: Strong widening-participation and access agreements (OfS-regulated) for low-income/first-gen students; casualised teaching remains a gap. Signal: regulated access program + casualisation.
rationale.BND: Announced major cuts with 900+ jobs at risk and 528 redundancies (highest in UK 2024-25), straining staff sustainability. Signal: large-scale redundancies during deficit.
rationale.ACC: Subject to OfS regulation and complaints processes; no recent pattern of proactive self-disclosed harm found. Signal: compliance-driven accountability posture.
rationale.SYS: Has published civic/social-responsibility strategy (a sector leader on social responsibility), but cuts undercut long-horizon wellbeing investment. Signal: strong stated social-responsibility mission vs. austerity.
rationale.INT: Stated social-responsibility values strained by deficit-driven redundancies; internal staff treatment seen as inconsistent with external messaging. Signal: values-vs-cuts tension under financial pressure.

rationale.AWR: Wellness Advisors do triage assessments; provincial Here2Talk 24/7 line plus grad-specific supports show multiple proactive channels. Signal: layered triage + 24/7 provincial counselling.
rationale.EMP: Grad student support groups and counselling are present; no independent ombuds caseload published, mixed grad accounts of being processed. Signal: grad support structures with uneven personal experience.
rationale.ACT: Stepped-care model (brief assessment then referral) and Here2Talk give fast first contact; deeper-care wait times less transparent. Signal: stepped-care fast entry, opaque depth-of-care waits.
rationale.EQU: Strong equity/Indigenous strategy and access programs; TA stipends (~$14.4-15k) sit below Vancouver cost of living. Signal: equity strategy vs. high-cost-of-living stipend gap.
rationale.BND: Unionised TAs (CUPE 2278) with collective agreement; health coverage exists but stipends strained by Vancouver housing costs. Signal: unionised TAs + cost-of-living pressure.
rationale.ACC: Has sexual-misconduct policy and reporting; historic SVPRO improvements followed scrutiny, but proactive disclosure limited. Signal: policy reforms partly reactive to past criticism.
rationale.SYS: Indigenous Strategic Plan and climate commitments show genuine long-horizon structural orientation. Signal: Indigenous Strategic Plan as structural commitment.
rationale.INT: Reasonable alignment between stated equity values and concrete plans; no major recent integrity scandal in evidence. Signal: published strategies broadly matched by action.

rationale.AWR: Student Wellness Hub and Office for Mediation and Reporting publish sexual-violence annual reports — real disaggregated detection on SV. Signal: OMR annual SV reporting.
rationale.EMP: 2024 pro-Palestinian encampment: McGill ended negotiations, threatened expulsions, called police (tear gas, 15 arrests) — punitive rather than empathic response. Signal: adversarial handling of student protest.
rationale.ACT: Wellness Hub stepped-care and AGSEM-covered TAs provide responses; bargaining conflict shows uneven action on worker need. Signal: functioning Hub amid labor conflict.
rationale.EQU: Equity offices and Indigenous initiatives exist; repeated AGSEM strikes (2024-25) over pay signal contingent-labor equity gaps. Signal: recurring grad-worker strikes over compensation.
rationale.BND: AGSEM TAs and new Unit 3 casuals unionised; but multiple strikes indicate unresolved labor-condition tension. Signal: unionised but contested labor relations.
rationale.ACC: OMR transparency on SV is a strength, but encampment response involved discipline before acknowledgment of student harm. Signal: SV transparency vs. punitive protest response.
rationale.SYS: Sustainability and Indigenous initiatives present; limited willingness to take institutionally risky structural positions during 2024 unrest. Signal: cautious structural posture.
rationale.INT: Calling police and pursuing expulsions during the encampment, after offering then withdrawing amnesty, signals values inconsistency under pressure. Signal: withdrawn amnesty + police action.

rationale.AWR: Counselling and wellbeing services plus a self-initiated employee-payments review that detected systemic underpayment — a genuine internal detection mechanism. Signal: self-detected $60M+ wage liability.
rationale.EMP: Student support exists; casual staff describe years of underpayment and impersonal treatment until remediation. Signal: casual-staff accounts of being processed.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services and a large remediation program (15,000+ staff repaid) show responsiveness once detected. Signal: large remediation rollout.
rationale.EQU: Widening-participation programs exist, but the casual-academic underpayment (~$62.4M owed) is a major equity failure toward lowest-paid workers. Signal: systemic casual underpayment.
rationale.BND: Heavy reliance on casualised academic labor with documented underpayment is a boundaries/exploitation failure. Signal: built-in casualisation + wage theft.
rationale.ACC: Self-disclosed underpayment, entered a Fair Work Enforceable Undertaking (Dec 2024), and is remediating — genuine accountability after detection. Signal: Fair Work Enforceable Undertaking + remediation.
rationale.SYS: Spent $12.3M on consultants while underpaying casuals raises questions about structural priorities. Signal: consultant spend vs. worker pay.
rationale.INT: Stated values undercut by years of underpayment; partly redeemed by transparent remediation, but the original gap reflects values inconsistency. Signal: long-running wage theft vs. stated values.

rationale.AWR: Dalida reporting system (extended 2024) for violence/harassment/discrimination received 35 reports, half SSV; structured detection channel. Signal: Dalida alert system live in 2024.
rationale.EMP: New VP for Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (2024) and doctoral-school mediation show real perspective-taking structures. Signal: dedicated EDI vice-presidency.
rationale.ACT: PhD students get occupational medicine + university health services; national Sante Psy Etudiant covers psychologist visits. Signal: dual health access + national psych scheme.
rationale.EQU: French public-fee model gives broad low-cost access; PhD-student precarity and inequities remain. Signal: low-fee access vs. doctoral precarity.
rationale.BND: PhD employee status gives some protection, but doctoral working-condition and harassment complaints persist. Signal: employee-status protections + ongoing harassment reports.
rationale.ACC: Dalida formalises harm reporting and appeal mechanisms in re-registration — structural acknowledgment pathways. Signal: formal reporting + appeal mechanism.
rationale.SYS: Federation structure pursues long-term research strategy; limited evidence of risky structural advocacy on student welfare. Signal: research-led strategy, modest welfare advocacy.
rationale.INT: EDI commitments appear genuine and recently strengthened; thinner English evidence limits verification (confidence flag). Signal: strengthened EDI vs. limited public verification.

rationale.AWR: Published harassment policy and counselling/health center exist; Japanese grad-student suicide data shows engineering/grad populations at elevated risk and detection is reactive. Signal: harassment policy + reactive detection.
rationale.EMP: Power-harassment (akahara/pawahara) culture in Japanese labs is a known structural empathy gap; formal complaint channels exist but personal validation is uneven. Signal: lab power-dynamic risk.
rationale.ACT: Counselling and harassment-consultation offices provide a response path; capacity and wait-time data not publicly benchmarked. Signal: consultation offices, opaque capacity.
rationale.EQU: Low female-faculty representation and limited disaggregated equity reporting are persistent gaps at Japanese flagships. Signal: gender-representation gap.
rationale.BND: Graduate researcher workload and supervisor dependency are high; limited public stipend/workload protections. Signal: high supervisor dependency.
rationale.ACC: Harassment policy provides a formal channel, but accountability transparency is limited in English-language evidence (confidence flag). Signal: formal channel, thin public outcome data.
rationale.SYS: Some national-level mental-health attention, but limited evidence of structural welfare advocacy carrying institutional risk. Signal: limited structural advocacy evidence.
rationale.INT: Kyoto has a tradition of academic-freedom independence; welfare-values consistency harder to verify in English (confidence flag). Signal: autonomy tradition vs. thin welfare evidence.

rationale.AWR: Counseling Center plus 'We Care' Title IX network provide multiple channels; GEO documents elevated grad-worker mental-health burden tied to working conditions. Signal: union-documented working-conditions distress.
rationale.EMP: Support structures exist, but grad-worker accounts describe pay/conditions causing distress, indicating uneven affective resonance. Signal: GEO accounts of conditions-driven distress.
rationale.ACT: Counseling Center and emergency-aid mechanisms function; wait-time benchmarking not consistently public. Signal: established counseling, opaque waits.
rationale.EQU: Strong land-grant access mission, Pell enrollment, and need-based aid; contingent and grad-worker pay equity contested. Signal: land-grant access vs. grad-pay disputes.
rationale.BND: Long-standing unionised GEO with collective agreements gives grad workers real protections and voice. Signal: established GEO bargaining.
rationale.ACC: Title IX office is documented; an external complaint (Equal Protection Project) shows scrutiny, with standard compliance accountability. Signal: active Title IX office + external complaints.
rationale.SYS: Land-grant public-good mission supports some upstream access work; limited evidence of risky structural advocacy. Signal: public-good mission, modest structural risk-taking.
rationale.INT: Public-university values broadly maintained, though grad-worker disputes reveal gaps between stated care and conditions. Signal: stated values vs. labor-condition gaps.

rationale.AWR: CMHC and Title IX office provide detection channels, but SB17 forced disbanding of equity offices that monitored marginalized-student distress. Signal: dismantled equity-monitoring infrastructure.
rationale.EMP: DEI ban led to closing the Division of Campus and Community Engagement, which supported most-struggling students — a structural empathy loss. Signal: closure of struggling-student support division.
rationale.ACT: Counseling and emergency aid continue, but capacity for marginalized populations weakened post-SB17. Signal: weakened differentiated support.
rationale.EQU: SB17 (effective Jan 2024) eliminated DEI programs, scrapped diversity requirement, and laid off ~49-60 staff — a direct equity regression. Signal: SB17-driven equity rollback.
rationale.BND: Large flagship with grad funding; no major wage-theft finding in evidence, but DEI-ban layoffs hit support staff. Signal: support-staff layoffs under state mandate.
rationale.ACC: Compliance with state law framed as required; limited proactive acknowledgment of harm to affected students/staff. Signal: state-mandated compliance over harm acknowledgment.
rationale.SYS: State political pressure pushed the institution away from structural equity work rather than toward it. Signal: structural retreat under political pressure.
rationale.INT: Rapidly dismantled stated diversity commitments under SB17 with limited public resistance — values abandoned under political pressure. Signal: DEI commitments dropped under legal/political pressure.

rationale.AWR: Counselling services exist; underpayment was detected via internal timesheet review (3.4M timesheets), showing a detection mechanism. Signal: large internal payroll review.
rationale.EMP: Student support present, but casual teachers reported being told to do unpaid student consultations — a clear empathy/respect failure toward workers. Signal: unpaid-consultation directive to casuals.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services plus an eight-figure back-pay remediation program show response after detection. Signal: landmark back-pay settlement.
rationale.EQU: Access programs exist, but ~$7.6M casual underpayment and Federal Court prosecution mark a worker-equity failure. Signal: $7.6M casual wage theft.
rationale.BND: Reliance on casual teaching associates with unpaid-work directives is a boundaries/exploitation failure. Signal: casualisation + unpaid labor.
rationale.ACC: Faced Federal Court prosecution (Dec 2024) and agreed to settle/back-pay — accountability under legal compulsion rather than purely self-initiated. Signal: court-driven settlement.
rationale.SYS: Has notable sustainability commitments (Net Zero), showing some long-horizon structural work. Signal: Net Zero structural commitment.
rationale.INT: Stated values undercut by 'unintentional' underpayment and unpaid-consultation directives; partial redemption via remediation. Signal: wage theth vs. stated values.

rationale.AWR: SNU Call suicide-prevention hotline (calls up 50% over 5 years) and a Human Rights Center show active detection; 43 student suicides in 20 years indicate persistent unmet need. Signal: SNU Call + Human Rights Center.
rationale.EMP: Power-harassment (gapjil) and a publicised professor sexual-abuse case point to empathy/validation gaps despite formal channels. Signal: gapjil culture + abuse case.
rationale.ACT: Suicide-prevention class and counselling expansion show responsiveness; capacity strained by rising demand. Signal: prevention programming under rising demand.
rationale.EQU: Elite-access concentration and limited disaggregated equity reporting are gaps; gender-equity issues raised via Human Rights Center cases. Signal: access concentration + equity cases.
rationale.BND: Hierarchical supervisor relationships create dependency/overwork risk for grad students. Signal: hierarchical lab dependency.
rationale.ACC: Human Rights Center provides a complaint pathway, but English-language outcome transparency is limited (confidence flag). Signal: complaint pathway, thin public outcomes.
rationale.SYS: Suicide-prevention programming shows some upstream orientation; limited evidence of risky structural advocacy. Signal: prevention focus, modest structural advocacy.
rationale.INT: Counselling investment suggests genuine concern, but recurring harassment cases reveal values-behavior gaps; thinner English evidence (confidence flag). Signal: investment vs. recurring harassment.

rationale.AWR: Mental-health support services plus a self-initiated casual-payment review (from 2019) detecting historical underpayment. Signal: self-initiated casual-pay review.
rationale.EMP: Student support present; casual academics report endemic underpayment and pressure to limit hours, indicating worker-empathy gaps. Signal: 'instructed to do a poor job' underpayment culture.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services and an active wage-remediation program show response after detection. Signal: ongoing remediation program.
rationale.EQU: Access programs exist, but sector-wide casual wage theft ($107.8M NTEU estimate) implicates UNSW's casualised workforce. Signal: casual-academic underpayment.
rationale.BND: Heavy casualisation with documented underpayment is a boundaries failure for contingent staff. Signal: casualisation + underpayment.
rationale.ACC: In regular contact with Fair Work Ombudsman and NTEU and remediating — accountability after detection. Signal: Fair Work + NTEU engagement.
rationale.SYS: Has a prominent sustainability/social-impact strategy (UNSW 2025); structural welfare advocacy less evidenced. Signal: social-impact strategy.
rationale.INT: Stated values strained by underpayment; remediation provides partial repair but original gap reflects inconsistency. Signal: underpayment vs. stated values.

rationale.AWR: Psychological Counselling Service for students and doctoral candidates plus a student-run Nightline provide layered detection. Signal: PCS + Nightline.
rationale.EMP: Dedicated doctoral Ombudsperson office (since 2011) acting as confidential impartial mediator shows structural empathy for grad students. Signal: standing doctoral ombudsperson.
rationale.ACT: PCS, Studentenwerk psychosocial counselling, and Graduate Academy support give multiple response paths. Signal: multiple counselling channels.
rationale.EQU: German low-fee public access is broadly equitable; doctoral and mid-career precarity (WissZeitVG fixed-term culture) is a German-sector equity gap. Signal: low-fee access vs. fixed-term precarity.
rationale.BND: Doctoral ombuds and Graduate Academy give some protection, but German fixed-term-contract norms create precarity. Signal: ombuds support amid fixed-term norms.
rationale.ACC: Confidential ombuds mediation provides a harm pathway; English-language outcome transparency limited (confidence flag). Signal: ombuds pathway, thin public outcomes.
rationale.SYS: Strong research-training infrastructure; limited evidence of risky structural welfare advocacy. Signal: research-training focus.
rationale.INT: Long-standing support structures suggest durable, non-performative care for researchers; thinner English evidence (confidence flag). Signal: durable support structures.

rationale.AWR: Doctoral ombuds as first contact for stress/work-life issues plus PhD-association mental-health sessions; Flemish region has run pioneering PhD mental-health surveys (the 'eye of the storm' study). Signal: doctoral ombuds + Flemish PhD wellbeing research culture.
rationale.EMP: Ombuds explicitly cover personal/stress/supervisor-communication issues, not just procedural disputes — genuine perspective-taking. Signal: broad-scope confidential ombuds.
rationale.ACT: Ombuds, ADS-funded grassroots mental-health initiatives, and peer sessions give multiple response paths. Signal: ADS-funded wellbeing initiatives.
rationale.EQU: Flemish low-fee access is broadly equitable; doctoral and contingent-researcher precarity remains. Signal: low-fee access vs. researcher precarity.
rationale.BND: Flemish OJO funding and doctoral ombuds support sustainability; fixed-term research culture still creates pressure. Signal: OJO funding + ombuds.
rationale.ACC: Ombuds provide confidential dispute resolution; English-language outcome transparency limited (confidence flag). Signal: confidential dispute resolution.
rationale.SYS: Active in European research-wellbeing discourse; limited evidence of institutionally risky structural advocacy. Signal: engagement in PhD-wellbeing field.
rationale.INT: Embedded ombuds/wellbeing structures suggest durable, non-performative care; thinner English governance evidence (confidence flag). Signal: embedded wellbeing structures.

rationale.AWR: UQ Counselling and faculty mental-health supports exist; underpayment was detected via internal pay-process review. Signal: internal pay-review detection.
rationale.EMP: Student support present; ~9,743 low-paid staff underpaid on average ~$800 during a cost-of-living crisis indicates worker-empathy failure. Signal: 9,743 staff underpaid in cost-of-living crisis.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services and remediation commitments provide response after detection. Signal: counselling + remediation.
rationale.EQU: Access programs exist, but a $7.88M casual/low-paid underpayment is a clear worker-equity failure. Signal: $7.88M underpayment.
rationale.BND: Lowest-paid casual and professional staff bore the underpayment, a boundaries/exploitation failure. Signal: lowest-paid staff underpaid.
rationale.ACC: Self-detected and disclosed the underpayment (May 2024) with NTEU scrutiny — accountability after internal detection. Signal: self-detected disclosure.
rationale.SYS: Has sustainability and research-impact strategies; structural welfare advocacy less evidenced. Signal: sustainability strategy.
rationale.INT: Stated values strained by systemic underpayment of its lowest-paid workers; remediation offers partial repair. Signal: underpayment vs. stated care.

rationale.AWR: Doctoral-college harassment guidance plus CROUS 24/7 anonymous psychological line and student-health services provide detection channels. Signal: doctoral harassment guidance + CROUS line.
rationale.EMP: Support exists; 11% of doctoral students dissatisfied and harassment/psychological-violence reports indicate empathy gaps for some. Signal: 11% doctoral dissatisfaction.
rationale.ACT: National Sante Psy Etudiant scheme (first visit free, GP referral dropped July 2024) plus university health service speed access. Signal: Sante Psy Etudiant access.
rationale.EQU: French public-fee model gives broad low-cost access; harassment (8% of CROUS calls) and discrimination (5.6%) show distribution gaps. Signal: low-fee access + reported harassment share.
rationale.BND: PhD-employee protections exist; doctoral work-life-balance and harassment issues persist. Signal: doctoral protections vs. persistent strain.
rationale.ACC: Formal harassment-reporting and signalement pathways exist; English-language outcome transparency limited (confidence flag). Signal: signalement pathways.
rationale.SYS: Benefits from national mental-health policy investment; institution-level structural advocacy less evidenced. Signal: national policy reliance.
rationale.INT: Support structures appear genuine and recently expanded; thinner English governance evidence (confidence flag). Signal: expanding support vs. limited verification.

rationale.AWR: Abrahart case found a deeply vulnerable disabled student received no direct contact from trained Student Services staff and policy non-compliance — a serious detection/awareness failure. Signal: coroner-found neglect, missed at-risk student.
rationale.EMP: Failure to make reasonable adjustments for a socially anxious student and to coordinate care reflects a structural empathy/perspective-taking failure. Signal: unmade reasonable adjustments.
rationale.ACT: Documented failure to act on a known-vulnerable student's needs (no timely intervention) is a direct responsiveness failure. Signal: failure to act on identified need.
rationale.EQU: UK widening-participation/access agreements exist and disability-discrimination finding spurs reform, but the disabled-student outcome was inequitable. Signal: access agreements vs. disability-equity failure.
rationale.BND: Student counselling and support services exist; cluster of student deaths prompted scrutiny of support adequacy. Signal: support services under scrutiny.
rationale.ACC: Bristol contested the negligence/Equality Act claim through 2024 rather than acknowledging harm early; High Court upheld liability — accountability came via litigation, not self-disclosure. Signal: liability upheld after years of contesting.
rationale.SYS: Case contributed to a national statutory-duty-of-care debate, but Bristol's own posture was defensive rather than structurally reforming-first. Signal: defensive litigation vs. sector reform momentum.
rationale.INT: Defending the case for years against a bereaved family, after a neglect finding, signals values-behavior inconsistency under legal/reputational pressure. Signal: prolonged defense after neglect finding.

rationale.AWR: Student Development Services psychological counselling and 'Caring for You' programs provide detection; campus mental-health demand is high post-2019. Signal: SDS counselling + caring programs.
rationale.EMP: Counselling exists, but HRW documents a climate of fear and self-censorship under the NSL that undermines genuine psychological safety. Signal: NSL chilling effect on candour.
rationale.ACT: Counselling services and HK-wide initiatives (including AI chatbots) give response paths; capacity vs. demand unclear. Signal: counselling + sector mental-health tools.
rationale.EQU: English-medium access is broad; equity work constrained by the political environment. Signal: broad access within constrained politics.
rationale.BND: Student support exists, but academic-freedom constraints limit free expression — a consent/autonomy concern for staff and students. Signal: NSL constraints on autonomy.
rationale.ACC: Accountability for political/academic-freedom harms is constrained by the NSL environment; institutional self-disclosure is unlikely. Signal: constrained accountability under NSL.
rationale.SYS: Mandatory national-security education and self-censorship indicate the institution is not positioned to critique structures harming its community. Signal: securitisation over structural critique.
rationale.INT: Operating within NSL pressures with documented self-censorship limits values-consistency on free inquiry and welfare; conduct-not-prestige scoring reflects this. Signal: self-censorship under NSL.

rationale.AWR: Wellness and Counselling Centre with a dedicated team, 24-hour hotline, and post-2019 trend studies (68.5% depressive symptoms) show active, data-informed detection. Signal: WACC + 24-hour hotline + cohort trend data.
rationale.EMP: Counselling team (psychologists, social workers) and peer-support training show real empathy infrastructure, though NSL climate constrains candour. Signal: multidisciplinary counselling + peer training.
rationale.ACT: Free counselling for all students, 24-hour immediate support, and mental-health training provide responsive, accessible action. Signal: free 24/7 student counselling.
rationale.EQU: English-medium broad access and mental-health programming; equity work constrained by political environment. Signal: broad access within constrained politics.
rationale.BND: Strong student support, but academic-freedom constraints (post-2019 siege legacy + NSL) limit autonomy/expression. Signal: support strength vs. NSL constraints.
rationale.ACC: After the 2019 campus siege, accountability for harms is shaped by a constrained political environment; self-disclosure unlikely. Signal: constrained post-2019 accountability.
rationale.SYS: Visible upstream mental-health investment (training, programming), but structural critique is limited by securitisation. Signal: mental-health investment vs. limited structural critique.
rationale.INT: Genuine, sustained counselling investment is a real strength; values-consistency on free inquiry constrained by NSL (conduct-not-prestige). Signal: durable counselling care vs. constrained inquiry.

rationale.AWR: After 2016 student suicides, CMU convened a Task Force on the CMU Experience and a Presidential Advisory Board, partnering with The Jed Foundation — a structured detection/assessment response. Signal: JED-partnered task force.
rationale.EMP: Support structures expanded, but 2025-26 Tartan reporting shows community criticism of the President's Advisory Board on Mental Health for lack of transparency. Signal: ongoing transparency criticism.
rationale.ACT: CaPS guarantees initial consultation within 5 business days, offers same-day urgent and 24/7 on-call, all at no cost — strong, benchmarked responsiveness. Signal: 5-day standard + same-day urgent.
rationale.EQU: Need-based aid and access efforts plus broad MHFA training; contingent-faculty and grad-pay equity less documented. Signal: aid + community MHFA training.
rationale.BND: Counselling capacity and crisis protocols are real; petitions/community pushes in 2025 signal unresolved demand pressure. Signal: capacity vs. ongoing student demand.
rationale.ACC: Title IX office and post-tragedy reviews show learning, but recent transparency criticism limits the accountability score. Signal: post-tragedy learning + transparency gaps.
rationale.SYS: Documented systemic mental-health reforms (limiting lethal means, social-connectedness programs, expanded CaPS staffing) show genuine root-cause orientation. Signal: structural mental-health reforms post-2016.
rationale.INT: Investment in wellbeing reforms is genuine, but persistent student criticism of advisory-board transparency reveals a values-execution gap. Signal: reform investment vs. transparency criticism.
